chapter_summary: Sūta narrates: having been praised by Kadru, the Lord whose vehicle bears Hari (Indra, the rain-bringer) covered the entire sky with dense masses of dark blue clouds. The clouds, ablaze with lightning and thundering ceaselessly against one another, poured down abundant water. The firmament seemed packed solid with these wondrous clouds raining incomparable torrents with mighty roars, and the sky appeared to dance with countless waves of rain as it filled with cloud-thunder. As Vāsava (Indra) sent down the rain, the Nāgas were filled with supreme joy, and the whole earth was flooded with water on every side.

story.title: Indra Brings the Rain for the Nāgas
story.arc: Praised by Kadru, Indra blankets the sky with thunderclouds and unleashes a tremendous downpour. The cosmic rainstorm—lightning, thunder, and ceaseless torrents—floods the earth and fills the serpent-Nāgas with supreme joy.
story.timeline_note: Follows Kadru's hymn of praise to Indra; the rain is the immediate response to that praise.
story.comic_potential: The mightiest of gods reduced to a celestial sprinkler-on-demand the moment Kadru flatters him—serpents cheering in the rain like kids let out for a monsoon holiday.

teachings.teaching: Sincere praise of the divine draws an immediate, abundant outpouring of grace—Kadru's hymn is answered at once by a sky-filling, earth-flooding rain.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework, devotional invocation opens the channel through which the higher power floods the practitioner; the deluge here mirrors the inrush of grace that answers turned-inward longing.

teachings.teaching: The same descending power that overwhelms the sky with storm is, to those it is meant for, pure life-giving joy—the Nāgas delight in the very deluge that floods the world.
teachings.lens_note: The downpour of higher energy is felt as turmoil from outside and as bliss by the one prepared to receive it; the Nāgas' joy is the inner ananda of the awakened current.
